results.possibleIdentity: Carolyn Brown (née Rice; born 1927) is a prominent American dancer, choreographer, and writer who was a central figure in the New York avant-garde art scene of the 1960s and 1970s. As a founding member and leading dancer of the Merce Cunningham Dance Company from 1953 to 1972, she was deeply embedded in the circle of artists who founded and promoted Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), including Robert Rauschenberg and John Cage. She performed in the landmark event '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering' in 1966, which served as the catalyst for the formal creation of E.A.T. Beyond her performance career, which she retired from in 1973, she has worked as a filmmaker, lecturer, and author, most notably writing the memoir 'Chance and Circumstance: Twenty Years with Cage and Cunningham' (2007).

Carolyn Brown is a Dallas-based photographer whose career has spanned over fifty years, beginning in the late 1960s and early 1970s. She is widely recognized for her documentary photography of architectural and archaeological sites in the Middle East (particularly Cairo and Palmyra), Mexico, and Central America. Her early artistic development included the study of ceramics and sculpture before she transitioned to photography in the 1970s while studying Islamic Art and Architecture at Cairo American University. Her work is held in major collections such as the Museum of Fine Arts, Houston, and the Los Angeles County Museum of Art (LACMA). While her primary focus is documentary photography, her recent work involves digital collage techniques, and her archive is preserved at the Edith O'Donnell Institute of Art History at the University of Texas at Dallas.

results.possibleIdentity: John Harris (born 1937) is an artist or engineer known for his collaboration with Fred Stern (born 1936) in the late 1960s. The duo, referred to as Harris & Stern, was associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization co-founded by Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg to foster collaborations between artists and the technical community. Harris & Stern participated in the landmark E.A.T. exhibition 'Some More Beginnings: An Exhibition of Submitted Works Involving Technical Materials and Processes,' which was held from November 1968 to February 1969 at the Brooklyn Museum and the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) in New York. This exhibition showcased more than 140 projects that integrated new technologies, such as lasers and electronic systems, into artistic practice.

L. John Harris is a Berkeley-based artist, illustrator, and writer who was active in the San Francisco Bay Area during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He studied art and literature at UC Berkeley in the 1960s and lived in an art commune in 1968. During this period, he was involved in collage-making and performance art before transitioning into a career as a journalist and food advocate. He is best known for his work during the 'Berkeley food revolution' of the 1970s, particularly the publication of 'The Book of Garlic' (1974) and his persona as 'Mr. Garlic.' His artistic career continued with the creation of 'Foodoodles' (culinary-themed cartoons) and more recent works such as his illustrated memoir 'Portrait in Red: A Paris Obsession' (2024).

results.possibleIdentity: Nam June Paik (1932–2006) was a Korean-American artist widely recognized as the "father of video art." He was highly active during the 1960s and 1970s and played a seminal role in bridging the gap between art, technology, and popular culture. Paik began his career in Germany as an avant-garde composer and a member of the Fluxus movement before moving to New York City in 1964. He is known for his innovative use of television sets, video recordings, and electronic music. In the late 1960s and early 1970s, he produced some of his most influential works, including 'Magnet TV' (1965), 'Robot K-456' (1964), and collaborations with cellist Charlotte Moorman such as 'TV Bra for Living Sculpture' (1969) and 'TV Cello' (1971). In 1969, he collaborated with engineer Shuya Abe to create the Paik-Abe Video Synthesizer, a device that allowed for real-time manipulation of video images. Paik was associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), the non-profit organization founded in 1966 to foster collaborations between artists and engineers; he was among the prominent figures of modern art who interacted with and participated in the activities of the group to explore the creative potential of new technologies. He is also credited with coining the term "electronic superhighway" in 1974 to describe the future of global telecommunications.

results.possibleIdentity: Davidson Gigliotti (born 1939) is an American video artist, art historian, and a pioneer of the early video art movement. Active since the late 1960s, he was an original member of the seminal alternative video collective Videofreex, founded in 1969, as well as the Media Bus collective. His artistic practice focuses on both single-channel tapes and multi-channel video installations that explore urban and rural environments, such as his notable 1972 work 'Quaking Aspens.' In 1975, Gigliotti became the Director of Video at the Experimental Intermedia Foundation in New York, a position he held until 1987. He is also recognized as an archivist and historian of the medium, having compiled extensive documentation and annotated lists of early video exhibitions (1963–1975). Gigliotti attended the Rhode Island School of Design and New York University and has received numerous accolades, including grants from the National Endowment for the Arts (NEA) and the Creative Artists Public Service (CAPS). His work is held in major collections such as the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) and the ZKM Center for Art and Media. While he is deeply embedded in the same New York scene as the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, his primary historical association is with the video collective movement that sought to democratize media and explore the sculptural qualities of video technology.

results.possibleIdentity: Susan Alexander is a dancer, choreographer, and educator who was active in the New York and California avant-garde scenes during the late 1960s and 1970s. She is best known for her long association with the Merce Cunningham Dance Company and School, where she served as a teacher until 1984. During the late 1960s, she led a dance residency at the University of California, Berkeley, an experience she later recalled for the backdrop of political protests and tear gas. Her career is closely tied to the circle of artists and engineers involved with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), as E.A.T. frequently collaborated with Merce Cunningham on technologically integrated performances such as 'RainForest' (1968). After her time in New York, she moved to France, where she spent over 20 years as a rehearsal director and teacher for the Paris Opera Ballet and the Paris Conservatory of Music and Dance. She eventually returned to California, settling in Santa Barbara in 2008 to continue teaching Cunningham-based modern dance.

Susan Alexjander (also known as Susan Alexander) is an American sound artist and composer recognized for her pioneering work at the intersection of art, science, and technology. Her practice involves translating biological and scientific data—such as the frequencies of DNA, atomic nuclei, and cosmic phenomena like black holes—into audible soundscapes using synthesizers and microtonal systems. She received her B.A. in 1966 and pursued a career that epitomizes the collaborative spirit of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement. Her work has been featured in major exhibitions exploring the 'Information Arts,' including collaborations with scientists to map the infrared absorption spectra of DNA molecules into music (notably in her 1990 album 'Sequencia'). Her soundscapes have accompanied installations by artists such as Rebecca Kamen, including projects presented at the Smithsonian Institution that utilize 'Larmor frequencies' from atomic nuclei. She has held teaching positions at San Jose State University and Goddard College and founded the 'Science & The Arts' initiative to further explore the musical properties of the universe.

results.possibleIdentity: Ira Schneider (1939–2022) was a pioneering American video artist and a central figure in the emergence of the video art movement during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is best known for his work in multi-channel video installation and his role as a co-founder of the Raindance Corporation (1969), an influential video collective and media think tank. Schneider co-founded and edited *Radical Software* (1970), the first journal dedicated to the burgeoning field of independent video and media ecology. One of his most significant artistic contributions was the multi-channel installation *Wipe Cycle* (1969), created in collaboration with Frank Gillette, which was featured in the landmark exhibition 'TV as a Creative Medium' at the Howard Wise Gallery in New York. This work utilized live camera feeds and delayed feedback to integrate the audience into the television environment. While frequently associated with the same historical milieu as Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), Schneider's primary focus was the 'Raindance' group and its pursuit of 'media ecology' and alternative television. He served as the president of the Raindance Foundation from 1972 to 1994 and co-edited the seminal book *Video Art: An Anthology* (1976) with Beryl Korot. His later work included major installations like *Manhattan is an Island* (1974) and *Time Zones* (1980). Schneider lived and worked in Berlin for several decades starting in 1993 before returning to New York in 2021 shortly before his death.

results.possibleIdentity: Richard Lowenberg (born August 31, 1946) is a multidisciplinary artist, designer, and eco-cultural activist whose career has bridged the gap between art, science, and technology since the 1960s. After studying architecture and design at Pratt Institute (1964–1968), he became deeply involved in the burgeoning New York City art and technology scene. In 1966, he served as a student assistant to Robert Rauschenberg for '9 Evenings: Theatre & Engineering,' the series of performances that led to the founding of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). He officially joined E.A.T. as a member in 1969 through Robert Whitman. Lowenberg was a founding member of the team that established The Kitchen in New York City in 1971, where he initiated its first Electronic Media Arts program. During the 1970s, he pioneered 'bio-sensing' art, using EEG, EMG, and other biotelemetric systems to translate biological signals from plants and animals into audio and video. Notable projects from this era include his role as the organizer of the bio-sensing performance for the film 'The Secret Life of Plants' (1976), an artist residency at NASA, and interspecies communication work with Koko the gorilla. His artistic practice, which he describes as an 'ecology of the information environment,' has also extended into tele-community planning and rural broadband initiatives through organizations like the Telluride Institute and the 1st-Mile Institute.
